content.problemStatement:
Given an array consisting of n integers, find the number of good subarrays.

Good subarray: A subarray alll, alll+1, alll+2, . . . , arrr−1, arrr is good if the sum of elements of this subarray is equal to the length of this subarray.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer NNN, denoting the length of the array.
The second line of each test case contains n integers AAA1, AAA2, . . . AAAN.


Output Format
Find the number of good subarrays
constraints: The answer fits in a 64‑bit signed integer
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the length of the array), the second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of good subarrays, i.e., subarrays whose sum equals their length.

solution.approach: For a subarray [l..r] we need sum(l..r)=r‑l+1. Let pref[i] be the sum of the first i elements (pref[0]=0). The condition becomes pref[r]-pref[l‑1]=r‑l+1 ⇔ (pref[r]-r) = (pref[l‑1]-(l‑1)). Define V[i]=pref[i]-i. Every pair of equal V values gives a good subarray. Thus we just need to count, for each distinct V, how many times it appears and add C(cnt,2). We can compute V on the fly and maintain a hash‑map of frequencies, adding the current frequency to the answer before incrementing it. This runs in O(N) per test case with O(N) extra space for the map.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) in the worst case for the hash‑map storing distinct V values.
